title: SEC Commissioner Sankey: Conference focused on federal legislation, not NCAA breakaway
dek: SEC Commissioner Greg Sankey said Wednesday that the conference prioritizes federal legislation over breaking away from the NCAA, though self-governance options remain under discussion. The SEC has weighed playing entirely conference games across all sports and warned that a full breakaway would trigger legal and congressional scrutiny.
author: The GoCheckMySports Desk
key_fact: Sankey stated the SEC's self-governance idea 'is not new' and 'it's not a breakaway' from the NCAA, while acknowledging that conversations about playing only conference games have occurred.
bottom_line: The SEC is publicly signaling preference for federal reform over independence, but the conference's active discussions about all-conference play and self-governance keep the breakaway option open as leverage in congressional negotiations. Watch whether Congress produces substantive college sports legislation this fall; if it stalls, the SEC's next statement on self-governance will reveal whether the independence threat is genuine.
body: SEC Commissioner Greg Sankey said Wednesday that the conference's focus is on improving college sports through federal legislation rather than separating from the NCAA, despite mounting frustrations across all athletic levels over governance and eligibility enforcement.

Sankey made the statement at SEC media days in Tampa, Florida, per ESPN's reporting. He acknowledged that frustrations are real but characterized the SEC's idea of self-governance as something that "is not new" and "is not a breakaway" from the NCAA. SEC leaders prefer getting help from Congress in the form of federal legislation, he indicated, citing persistent challenges the NCAA faces in enforcing player eligibility rules.

Yet the conference has not closed the door on independence. According to ESPN's reporting, multiple sources confirmed that the SEC has had additional conversations about considering playing entirely conference games in all sports. In early March, SEC commissioners and chancellors met in Nashville, Tennessee to discuss self-governance options if Congress does not enact federal legislation. Sankey acknowledged these discussions but framed them as contingency planning rather than preference.

Sankey pointed to eligibility enforcement as a specific pain point, noting that the NCAA's reliance on waivers and lack of clarity on eligibility rules has prompted questions about whether the SEC should establish more specific rules of its own. He warned that a full conference breakaway would have broader national implications, including potential legal problems. If the SEC pursued such a move, Sankey said, "the SEC would be in front of Congress explaining themselves."

Sankey also addressed cultural and competitive concerns about independence. He stated that American culture expects March Madness to happen, with Cinderella stories and 30 automatic bids to exist, implying that breaking away could jeopardize those traditional tournament structures. Sankey noted that HBCUs in the SEC footprint are attached to Division I and that he communicates with his SWAC colleague regularly, suggesting the league is mindful of broader collegiate sports ecosystem effects.
Sankey indicated the SEC will consider its position based on legislation it can review, with a decision to settle for federal legislation or move forward without it.
